title: Individual Oral — analysis and delivery
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to mastering the IB English A: Language and Literature HL Individual Oral (IO). It focuses on developing sophisticated textual analysis (Criterion B) and delivering a persuasive, organised presentation (Criteria C & D), moving beyond surface-level commentary to meet top-band assessment requirements.
learningObjectives: Analyse and evaluate how authorial choices in literary and non-literary texts present a chosen global issue.
Structure a focused, coherent, and well-balanced oral, using signposting to guide the listener.
Employ precise, varied, and effective language appropriate for formal literary and linguistic analysis.
Deliver a persuasive and engaging oral presentation, demonstrating confident knowledge and understanding.
Respond to follow-up questions with flexibility, accuracy, and insight, demonstrating a wider knowledge of the works.

simpleExplanation.title: The IO: Your Literary Tour Guide
simpleExplanation.summary: The Individual Oral is a 10-minute presentation, followed by a 5-minute Q&A, where you analyse one literary and one non-literary text through the lens of a 'global issue'. It's not a memorised speech, but a structured, expert conversation about how texts create meaning.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you are a specialist tour guide in a museum. Your two 'exhibits' are your chosen extracts. Your 'tour group' is the examiner. Your job is not just to say 'Here is a metaphor'. Your job is to guide the examiner's attention to that metaphor, explain the artist's (author's) technique, and reveal how this specific choice illuminates the museum's central theme (your global issue). Your delivery should be as engaging as your insights.
steps: Select & Connect: Choose a literary work and a non-literary body of work you have studied. Find a compelling 'global issue' that links them. Select a specific, rich extract (or text) from each that powerfully represents this issue.

Analyse & Deconstruct: Annotate your extracts, identifying specific authorial choices (stylistic, structural, etc.). For each choice, ask: 'How does this shape meaning?' and 'How does this meaning connect to my global issue?'

Structure & Outline: Create a 10-bullet-point outline (the maximum allowed). Plan a clear introduction, balanced analysis of both extracts, and a concluding synthesis. Use your points as signposts, not a full script.

Rehearse & Refine: Practise delivering your oral, timing yourself to hit the 10-minute mark. Focus on a natural, engaging tone, clear articulation, and purposeful pacing. Anticipate questions and prepare to discuss the works more broadly.

flashcards.front: Criterion A: Knowledge, Understanding and Interpretation
flashcards.back: Assesses your understanding of the extracts, the whole works, and your ability to interpret how they relate to the chosen global issue. Top marks require 'excellent' knowledge and 'perceptive' interpretation.

Assesses your ability to analyse and evaluate how authorial choices (stylistic and structural) construct meaning. Top marks are for analysis that is 'insightful,' 'convincing,' and 'well-supported' by the extracts.
Assesses the logical structure and coherence of your oral. Top marks require a 'well-focused' and 'highly effective' structure, with a clear balance between the two works.
Assesses the clarity, accuracy, and effectiveness of your language. Top marks are for language that is 'clear, varied, precise,' and 'consistently effective,' including subject-specific terminology.
A significant, wide-ranging issue that has a local, national, and global dimension. It must be the central thread connecting your analysis of both the literary and non-literary works.
Deliberate decisions made by a creator to shape meaning. This includes stylistic features (diction, imagery, tone) and structural features (form, layout, narrative perspective, enjambment).
A key principle of the IO. Your claims must be rooted in specific evidence from your chosen extracts. Avoid making broad, unsupported generalisations about the work as a whole.

Using transitional phrases to guide the examiner through your argument. E.g., 'Turning now to the non-literary text...', 'This idea is further complicated by...', 'A similar technique can be observed in...'.
Varying your vocal tone, pitch, and pace during delivery to add emphasis, convey emotion, and maintain listener engagement. A key component of a persuasive performance.
Simply identifying literary or stylistic devices (e.g., 'There is a metaphor here.') without analysing their specific effect on meaning in relation to the global issue.
You are not required to do a point-by-point comparison. Instead, you should synthesise: show how both texts, in their unique ways, illuminate different facets of the same global issue.
The maximum number of points allowed on your outline sheet. They must not be full sentences or extensive notes. Use them as a structural guide and memory aid only.

faq.q: How do I choose a good global issue?
faq.a: Choose an issue that genuinely interests you and is clearly and complexly represented in both your chosen works. A good global issue is specific enough to be manageable but broad enough to be significant. Instead of 'War', try 'The representation of civilian trauma in conflict'. Instead of 'Love', try 'The commodification of romance in consumer culture'.

faq.q: Is it okay to memorise my oral?
faq.a: No. Examiners can easily spot a memorised speech, which often sounds robotic and lacks genuine engagement. This will negatively impact your score in Criterion D. Furthermore, it makes you less flexible in the Q&A. Practise extensively with your bullet-point outline so you know your material inside-out, but allow the exact wording to be natural and spontaneous.

faq.q: What if I run out of time or finish too early?
faq.a: Timing is why practice is essential. If you are running short, focus on your most important analytical point. If you finish with a minute or two to spare, do not panic. Use the time to offer a more developed concluding synthesis, perhaps drawing one final, powerful connection between your two texts. It is better to finish slightly early with a complete argument than to be cut off mid-sentence.

faq.q: How much should I talk about the work as a whole versus the extract?
faq.a: Your analysis must be firmly rooted in the extract (Criterion B). This should form the bulk of your 10-minute presentation. However, you should make brief, judicious references to the wider work to show your comprehensive knowledge (Criterion A). The Q&A is the primary opportunity to demonstrate your understanding of the whole work, so be prepared to discuss themes, characters, and structural elements beyond your specific extract.

sections.content: The Individual Oral (IO) is a cornerstone of the English A: Language and Literature HL course, accounting for 20% of your final grade. It is your opportunity to demonstrate the full range of your analytical skills in a live, structured conversation. This lesson will equip you with the strategies to move beyond competent analysis and deliver an insightful, persuasive, and highly-scored oral presentation that showcases your deep engagement with the texts.
Deconstructing the Assessment Criteria: What Examiners Reward

Understanding the markbands is the first step to success. Examiners are not just listening for good ideas; they are measuring your performance against four specific criteria. A top-band performance is 'perceptive', 'insightful', 'convincing', and 'effective' across the board.

items: Criterion A (Knowledge & Interpretation): Demonstrate you know the works inside-out, not just the extracts. Your interpretation of how the global issue is presented must be nuanced and well-argued, not obvious or superficial.

Criterion B (Analysis & Evaluation): This is the engine of your IO. Do not just identify techniques. You must explain precisely *how* and *why* an author's choice of language, structure, or style shapes the audience's understanding of the global issue. The key is linking the micro (a word choice) to the macro (the global issue).

Criterion C (Focus & Organisation): A logical flow is non-negotiable. Your oral must have a clear beginning, middle, and end. It should be well-balanced, giving roughly equal time to each work, and use clear transitions (signposting) to ensure the examiner can follow your line of argument effortlessly.

Criterion D (Language): Use the vocabulary of a literary and linguistic critic. This means employing terms like 'asyndeton', 'focalisation', 'modality', or 'juxtaposition' correctly and purposefully. Your expression should be clear, fluent, and precise, avoiding colloquialisms and filler words.

sections.content: The difference between a 6 and a 10 in Criterion B lies in the depth of analysis. Lower-scoring responses describe what is happening in the text and list features. Higher-scoring responses explain *how* these features function to create complex meanings and actively persuade the audience. Your analysis must always serve your central argument about the global issue. A useful framework is Point -> Evidence -> Analysis -> Link to Global Issue.

sections.question: Analyse how authorial choices in this extract from Kazuo Ishiguro's *Never Let Me Go* contribute to an exploration of the global issue 'Beliefs, values and education'. The extract describes the students at Hailsham being told about their fate as organ donors.
sections.solution:
In this model paragraph of an oral, notice the integration of evidence, the focus on authorial choice, and the explicit link to the global issue:

'Ishiguro's exploration of how education systems can enforce societal values is powerfully rendered through the deliberately flat and unemotional tone of Miss Lucy's revelation. When she states, 'Your lives are set out for you,' the choice of the passive voice is crucial. It removes agency not only from the students but also from the educators, suggesting a systemic, inescapable structure of belief that is simply 'is,' rather than being actively imposed by individuals. This linguistic choice constructs a reality where the value system of donation is presented as a natural fact, not a debatable ethic. Furthermore, the narrator Kathy's understated reaction—focusing on the mundane details of the classroom rather than the horror of the news—highlights the success of this educational model. Her internalised acceptance, a product of years of conditioning, demonstrates how deeply ingrained values can supersede fundamental human beliefs about life and self-preservation. Therefore, Ishiguro uses narrative perspective and passive syntax not just to tell us about this dystopian education, but to make the audience feel the chilling effectiveness of a value system that has been completely and successfully internalised.'

sections.content: Always ask 'So what?'. You've identified a polysyndetic list. So what? How does the repetitive use of 'and' create a sense of breathlessness or overwhelming scale? And how does that feeling connect to your global issue of, for example, 'the ethics of consumerism'?
Structuring for Clarity and Impact (Criterion C)

A brilliant analysis can be lost in a confusing structure. A well-organised oral guides the examiner logically through your argument, making your insights easy to follow and appreciate. While there is no single mandatory structure, a clear, balanced approach is essential. Your 10 bullet points are your roadmap.

items: Introduction (approx. 1 min): Briefly introduce your literary work, non-literary body of work, and the specific extracts. State your global issue clearly and provide a 'thesis' for your oral – what is the overall argument you will make about how these texts explore the issue?
Body Paragraph 1: Literary Text (approx. 4 mins): Analyse your literary extract. Focus on 2-3 key authorial choices and link each one back to the global issue. Integrate quotations smoothly.

Body Paragraph 2: Non-Literary Text (approx. 4 mins): Transition clearly ('Turning now to the advertisement campaign...'). Analyse your non-literary text, again focusing on 2-3 key choices and their connection to the global issue. Ensure your analysis is balanced in depth and time with the literary text.

Conclusion (approx. 1 min): Briefly synthesise your findings. Do not just summarise. Offer a concluding thought on what these two texts, when considered together, reveal about the complexity of the global issue. This is your final chance to sound insightful.

sections.content: Mastering Delivery and the Q&A (Criterion D & A)

Your delivery is not just about speaking clearly; it is about performing your knowledge. An engaged, confident tone is persuasive. Use your bullet points as a safety net, not a script. Make eye contact with your teacher. Use vocal modulation to emphasise key analytical points. The 5-minute Q&A is not an afterthought; it is a chance to demonstrate the breadth of your knowledge (Criterion A). Listen carefully to the question and provide a considered, specific response that refers back to the texts.

sections.question: The student has just presented on a series of Banksy's street art and the novel *The Handmaid's Tale*, exploring the global issue 'Art, creativity and imagination'. The examiner asks: 'You focused on the visual symbolism in one Banksy piece. How does the ephemeral nature of his work as a whole contribute to his political message?'
sections.solution:
This model response demonstrates flexibility and knowledge beyond the chosen extract:

'That's an excellent question, as the temporary nature of Banksy's street art is fundamental to its power. While my chosen piece, 'Girl with Balloon', uses simple visual symbolism, its impact is magnified by its context. By placing his art on public walls, Banksy ensures it's temporary—it can be painted over, cleaned off, or destroyed. This ephemerality acts as a powerful metaphor for the fragility of hope and protest itself, which directly links to the global issue of art as a form of resistance. Unlike a permanent gallery piece, its transient nature suggests that the message must be seen and understood *now*. This urgency mirrors the situation in *The Handmaid's Tale*, where Offred's small acts of rebellion, like her internal thoughts or the secret message scratched in the wardrobe, are equally fleeting and dangerous. Both artists, then, use the very form and medium of their work—be it illegal street art or a secret narrative—to argue that creative expression under oppressive systems is often a temporary, high-stakes act, making it all the more significant.'

items.label: IB Academy English A IO Guide
